skill.name: xlsx
skill.description: Create, edit, analyze, format, and verify spreadsheet files with Python, pandas, openpyxl, and LibreOffice recalculation workflows.
skill.summary: Work with Excel and tabular files while preserving formulas, formatting, and calculation checks.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from markdown examples, spreadsheet cell references, and license URLs. The confirmed risk is local execution and filesystem modification by recalc.py, which installs a LibreOffice macro and runs fixed external commands. No evidence found for prompt injection, data exfiltration, command injection, or confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: LibreOffice Helper Runs External Programs
medium_findings.description: recalc.py invokes soffice, timeout, and gtimeout through subprocess.run. The commands use argument arrays and no shell, so direct command injection is not evident, but running external office software on workbook files creates a real local execution and dependency risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are directly present and semantically part of the formula recalculation workflow. Confidence is not higher because arguments are fixed arrays and no shell expansion is used.

medium_findings.title: Persistent LibreOffice Macro Installation
medium_findings.description: The helper writes Module1.xba into the user's LibreOffice profile under a hidden configuration directory. This is intended for recalculation, but it modifies persistent application state outside the target workbook.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code constructs a hidden LibreOffice profile path, creates the directory, and writes a macro file. The behavior is clear, though the macro content itself is limited to recalculation and saving.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Not Network Calls
low_findings.description: The license file contains legal terms URLs, and recalc.py contains an XML namespace URL inside macro text. I did not find evidence that these URLs are fetched or used for data transfer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are static text or XML metadata, not request logic. No network client code is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md external command findings occur inside markdown prose, code fences, or inline code examples. They document spreadsheet workflows and do not execute when the skill file is read.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static hits are in markdown code spans and fenced examples. The only executable implementation is recalc.py, which is assessed separately.

low_findings.title: C2 Keyword Findings Are Spreadsheet Cell References
low_findings.description: The C2 matches in SKILL.md are Excel cell references inside formula examples. I did not find evidence of command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is an Excel formula example using cell C2. The surrounding section explains spreadsheet formulas.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited SKILL.md lines describe spreadsheet behavior, documentation requirements, pandas usage, or Python calculation examples. I did not find evidence of weak cryptographic algorithms at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text contains spreadsheet and Python examples, not hashing or encryption calls. The finding appears to be substring matching rather than semantic cryptography use.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: The cited lines are Excel error names, pandas read options, and code style guidance. I did not find host reconnaissance commands or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are documentation content, not code that queries the host. There is no evidence of reconnaissance intent in the cited section.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Executes Office Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper launches LibreOffice and timeout utilities from Python. This should be disclosed because it depends on local binaries and processes user-selected spreadsheet files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The executable calls are confirmed, but the implementation avoids shell=True and does not concatenate user input into a command string.

dangerous_patterns.title: Writes to Hidden Application Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper creates and writes files in the LibreOffice user configuration directory. This persistent change is broader than editing the requested spreadsheet file.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are explicit and target a hidden application profile. The macro purpose appears functional rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet work often loses formulas, formats, and validation checks during automated edits. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows for creating, editing, analyzing, and recalculating workbook files.
actual_capabilities: Read Excel, CSV, and TSV data with pandas for analysis.
Create new workbook files with formulas, formatting, and sheets.
Modify existing Excel files while preserving formulas and styles.
Recalculate workbook formulas through LibreOffice using recalc.py.
Scan recalculated files for common Excel formula errors.
Apply finance-oriented formatting conventions for models.
limitations: Formula recalculation requires LibreOffice and local command execution.
The helper may write a macro into the user's LibreOffice profile.
Very large workbooks can be slow because all cells may be scanned.
It does not replace manual review for business logic or model assumptions.

use_cases.title: Build Financial Models
use_cases.description: Create projection sheets with formulas, assumptions, formatting, and formula error checks.

use_cases.title: Clean Operational Data
use_cases.description: Load tabular files, inspect columns, normalize data, and export a formatted workbook.

use_cases.title: Update Existing Templates
use_cases.description: Modify workbook content while preserving existing layout, formulas, and workbook conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this spreadsheet and summarize its sheets, columns, formulas, and visible data quality issues.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Formatted Workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Excel workbook from this data with clear headers, useful formats, formulas for totals, and a summary sheet.

prompt_templates.title: Modify a Template Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this existing workbook with the new inputs. Preserve its current formatting, formulas, sheet names, and layout conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Recalculate and Diagnose
prompt_templates.prompt: Recalculate this workbook, check for formula errors, identify affected sheets and cells, and propose precise fixes.

output_examples.input: A sales workbook needs a monthly summary and formula checks.
output_examples.output: A revised workbook with a summary sheet, monthly totals, consistent formatting, and a list of formula issues found.

output_examples.input: A CSV export has mixed date formats and missing department values.
output_examples.output: A cleaned spreadsheet with normalized dates, highlighted missing fields, and a short data quality summary.

output_examples.input: A finance template needs updated revenue assumptions.
output_examples.output: An updated model that preserves template styling, uses linked assumption cells, and reports recalculation status.

best_practices: Keep assumptions in separate cells and reference them from formulas.
Recalculate workbooks after formula edits and review reported errors.
Preserve existing workbook conventions when editing user templates.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode calculated values when a formula should stay dynamic.
Do not save a formula workbook after loading only calculated values.
Do not run recalculation helpers on untrusted workbook files without review.

faq.question: What file types does this skill support?
faq.answer: It focuses on Excel workbooks and tabular files, including XLSX, XLSM, CSV, and TSV files.

faq.question: Can it preserve existing spreadsheet formatting?
faq.answer: Yes. The instructions emphasize preserving existing templates, styles, formulas, sheet names, and conventions.

faq.question: Does it calculate Excel formulas by itself?
faq.answer: No. It uses LibreOffice through recalc.py to recalculate formula values in workbook files.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The helper script runs local programs and writes a LibreOffice macro into a user configuration directory.

faq.question: Is there evidence of malicious network activity?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The URL findings are legal links and an XML namespace, not network requests.

faq.question: Can it replace spreadsheet review by a domain expert?
faq.answer: No. It can check formulas and structure, but business assumptions still need expert review.
